cookies.title: Cookie Policy
cookies.what_body: We use only essential cookies: authentication cookies to keep you signed in and a language cookie so the interface stays in your chosen language.
cookies.how_body: Cookies are set at login and refreshed during navigation; they are scoped to /api, marked HttpOnly, and not used for advertising.
cookies.manage_body: You can clear cookies in your browser at any time. Blocking auth cookies will sign you out and may prevent navigation logging.
cookies.necessary_body: Auth and language cookies are required for the service to work (login sessions, CSRF protection, localization). Consent is not requested for these.
cookies.research_body: Navigation and query events are logged to improve the learning experience and for research, as described in the privacy notice.

privacy.title: Privacy Notice
privacy.data_body: Account details (username, role, school), authentication cookies, app navigation events, SQL queries and feedback you submit, and technical logs for security.
privacy.use_body: To provide the service (authentication, exercises), measure performance, improve learning features, and conduct research on SQL education.
privacy.disclosure_body: Data stays within the research team and hosting providers bound by contract. We do not sell personal data or use third-party trackers.
privacy.rights_body: You can request access or deletion of your account data via email. Deleting may remove progress. Essential cookies are required for login.

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.use_body: Use the platform for learning SQL. Do not upload unlawful, confidential, or malicious content, and do not attempt to disrupt the service.
terms.account_body: Keep your credentials confidential. You are responsible for activity under your account.
terms.content_body: Queries and feedback you submit may be logged and used for research and improving the service, as described in the privacy notice.
terms.liability_body: The service is provided “as is” without warranties. We may modify or suspend it. To the fullest extent permitted by law, liability is limited to the maximum allowed.
terms.contact_body: For questions about these terms: davide.ponzini@edu.unige.it

tips: Include the whole script into a BEGIN; ... COMMIT; block
Begin the script with DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS schema_name CASCADE;
Use a unique search_path for each dataset
Group insertions into a single INSERT INTO statement for each table to greatly improve performance

about.title: About LensQL
about.p1: LensQL is an interactive platform that helps students learn SQL through guided practice, personalized feedback, and gamified progression. Built with both students and teachers in mind, it supports a deeper understanding of relational databases by focusing on the reasoning behind queries — not just their correctness.
about.p2: Instead of offering immediate solutions, LensQL encourages active exploration. The AI assistant, Lens, helps students reflect on their mistakes, refine their logic, and grow their skills with every attempt. Teachers, meanwhile, gain insight into how students learn, where they struggle, and how to support them effectively.
about.p3: Whether you're running your first query or refining complex joins, LensQL turns every error into an opportunity to learn.
about.moreIntro: LensQL is developed as part of ongoing research at the University of Genoa. If you're curious about the academic background or technical details, here are some publications that explore the ideas behind it:
about.paper1: Analyzing Common Student Errors in SQL Query Formulation to Enhance Learning Support
about.paper1Desc: A large-scale study on student SQL mistakes and how they vary across learning settings.
about.paper2: Enhancing SQL Learning through Generative AI and Student Error Analysis
about.paper2Desc: A framework for AI-assisted feedback, error tracking, and personalized assignment generation in SQL education.

prompts.describe: What does my query do? Provide a concise summary of its behavior.
prompts.explain: What does my query do in detail? Explain what each clause in my query is doing.
prompts.explain_error: What does this error mean? Explain the error message in simple terms.
prompts.example: What can cause this error? Provide a simplified example query that could lead to this error.
prompts.locate: Where is the problem in my query? Show me which query part is causing this error.
prompts.fix: How can I fix this error? Suggest a possible correction for this error.
prompts.detect_errors: Does my query have any errors? Analyze my query and identify any potential issues.

hero.title: Learn SQL Through Mistakes – With LensQL
hero.subtitle: Discover SQL. Learn from your errors. Grow with Lens.
hero.description: LensQL is an AI-powered SQL learning platform that turns student mistakes into personalized, interactive learning moments.

what_is.title: What is LensQL?
what_is.subtitle: A smarter way to learn SQL — grounded in your mistakes, powered by AI.

features.ai_tutor: 🤖 AI-driven tutor that helps students debug queries and think critically
features.error_pedagogy: 🧠 Error-based pedagogy: Learn from syntax, logic, and semantic mistakes
features.personalization: 🎯 Personalized assignments and dashboards based on real progress

what_is.quote: “Over 95% of queries are submitted outside class hours. LensQL supports self-paced, reflective learning.”

how_it_works.title: How Does LensQL Work
steps: 🧑‍🏫 Teachers create exercises, optionally targeting known error types
🎓 Students solve queries with Lens’ help — no direct answers, only interactive guidance
📊 The system analyzes every query to extract error patterns and track progress
🛠️ Personalized exercises are automatically generated to address specific weaknesses
📈 Interactive learning dashboards show students and teachers how understanding evolves over time

meet_lens.title: Meet Lens: Your SQL Sidekick
meet_lens.description: Lens used to be an explorer of ancient data relics — until a mysterious SQL query turned them into an AI. Now, Lens guides students through the twists and the joins of query logic with curiosity, kindness, and clear feedback.

features.describe_query: 🧭 Describes your query's behavior to help reflect on intent vs result
features.suggest_after_reflection: ⚠️ Gives suggestions only after reflection — to foster genuine learning

students.title: For Students
items: 🧠 Understand the why, not just the how

gamification.title: 🎮 Gamified Learning That Rewards Effort and Insight
gamification.description: Learning SQL should feel rewarding — and a little fun, too. LensQL uses two progression systems that motivate students to practice, reflect, and grow.

xp.title: 🔹 XP and Levels — Progress Through Practice
xp.description: Earn experience by writing and running queries. Leveling up shows your growth over time and rewards consistent effort — not just correct answers.
xp.quote: “You don’t need to be perfect — just consistent. The more you experiment, the more you grow.”

coins.title: 🪙 LensCoins — Use Wisely, Reflect Deeply
coins.description: LensCoins are earned through engagement and spent when asking Lens for help. They encourage students to try before asking — and to reflect on feedback.
coins.quote: “LensCoins encourage reflection. Spend them when you're truly stuck — and earn them back by solving more and helping Lens improve.”

why.title: 💡 Why It Works
items: 🤝 Builds a dialogue between students and the AI assistant
🔄 Helps teachers see who’s trying, not just who’s succeeding

students.title: For Students
items: 📌 Track how many queries you’ve run and how often they work
📊 Understand what types of queries you write and how they change over time
🤖 See when and why you interact with Lens, and how you respond to its suggestions
🕒 Follow your learning progress with a timeline of queries and errors

teachers.title: For Teachers
items: 📊 View aggregate metrics for each dataset: query activity, success rate, and help usage
📂 Analyze errors by category: syntax, semantic, logical, or complications
🕒 Track how query behavior and error patterns evolve over time
🧭 Adapt instruction based on what students try, not just what they get right

demo.title: 🚀 Try LensQL Now
demo.description: While LensQL requires registration, here’s a sneak peek of how it helps you reason about errors step by step.
demo.example_caption: Example: SELECT name FROM students WHERE grade > AVG(grade)
demo.example_note: Lens identifies a syntax error and explains it in plain language, without giving the answer.

community.description: Already helping 100+ students outside the classroom.

Simple Select.label: Simple SELECT
Simple Select.description: Write a simple SELECT statement to retrieve data from a single table.

Single condition.label: Single condition
Single condition.description: Write queries that filter results based on a single condition.

Distinct.label: DISTINCT
Distinct.description: Use the DISTINCT keyword to eliminate duplicate rows from query results.

Aggregate functions.label: Aggregate functions
Aggregate functions.description: Use aggregate functions like COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX to summarize data.

Subquery in SELECT.label: Subquery in SELECT
Subquery in SELECT.description: Write queries that include subqueries in the SELECT clause.

Case statements.label: CASE statements
Case statements.description: Use CASE statements to create conditional logic within queries.

String functions.label: String functions
String functions.description: Apply string functions to manipulate text data in queries.

Date functions.label: Date functions
Date functions.description: Use date functions to manipulate and format date and time data in queries.

Numeric functions.label: Numeric functions
Numeric functions.description: Apply numeric functions to perform calculations on numeric data in queries.

Window functions.label: Window functions
Window functions.description: Use window functions to perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row.

Simple join.label: Simple JOIN
Simple join.description: Write queries that involve a simple join between two tables to retrieve related data.

Self join.label: Self JOIN
Self join.description: Write queries that involve self-joins to compare rows within the same table.

Left join.label: LEFT JOIN
Left join.description: Write queries that use LEFT JOIN to retrieve all rows from one table and matching rows from another.

Right join.label: RIGHT JOIN
Right join.description: Write queries that use RIGHT JOIN to retrieve all rows from one table and matching rows from another.

Full outer join.label: FULL OUTER JOIN
Full outer join.description: Use FULL OUTER JOIN to retrieve all rows when there is a match in either table.

Subquery in FROM.label: Subquery in FROM
Subquery in FROM.description: Utilize subqueries in the FROM clause to create derived tables.

Multiple conditions.label: Multiple conditions
Multiple conditions.description: Use logical operators to filter results based on multiple conditions.

Subquery in WHERE.label: Subquery in WHERE
Subquery in WHERE.description: Use subqueries in the WHERE clause to filter results based on related data.

Correlated subqueries.label: Correlated subqueries
Correlated subqueries.description: Write correlated subqueries that reference columns from the outer query.

Group by.label: GROUP BY
Group by.description: Group results using the GROUP BY clause to aggregate data based on specific columns.

Group by with conditions.label: HAVING clause
Group by with conditions.description: Use the HAVING clause to filter grouped results based on aggregate conditions.

Order by.label: ORDER BY
Order by.description: Sort query results using the ORDER BY clause.

Limit and offset.label: LIMIT and OFFSET
Limit and offset.description: Use LIMIT and OFFSET to control the number of rows returned by a query.

Union.label: UNION
Union.description: Combine results from multiple queries using the UNION operator.

Intersect.label: INTERSECT
Intersect.description: Retrieve common results from multiple queries using the INTERSECT operator.

Except.label: EXCEPT
Except.description: Use the EXCEPT operator to find results in one query that are not in another.

syn.name: Syntax Errors
syn.description: Incorrect SQL syntax, such as missing keywords, incorrect punctuation, or malformed statements.

sem.name: Semantic Errors
sem.description: Correct syntax but query is meaningless, such as always returning an empty result set.

log.name: Logical Errors
log.description: Query runs without errors but produces incorrect results due to flawed logic or misunderstanding of requirements.

com.name: Complications
com.description: Query returns the correct result but uses inefficient methods or complex constructs that could be simplified.

1.name: Ambiguous column
1.description: When using multiple tables in a query, a column present in more than one table is referenced without specifying which table it belongs to.
1.explanation: Both tables contain a column named `city`. Without qualifying the table, the database cannot determine which `city` column to use.

2.name: Ambiguous function
2.description: Multiple functions with the same name exist, and the query does not specify which one to use.
2.explanation: There are two functions named `my_function`, one that takes an interval and another that takes a time. The query does not specify which function to use, leading to ambiguity.

3.name: Undefined column
3.description: The query references a column that does not exist in the specified table(s).
3.explanation: The table customer does not contain a column named `credit_card`.

4.name: Undefined function
4.description: The query references a function that does not exist or is not defined.
4.explanation: The function `CAPSLOCK` does not exist in the database.

5.name: Undefined parameter
5.description: The query references a parameter that does not exist or is not defined. Also, placeholder parameters are used incorrectly.
5.explanation: The query uses placeholder parameters instead of actual values. Even though this can be valid syntax in prepared statements, it is not in regular queries.

6.name: Undefined object
6.description: The query references a table that does not exist or is not defined.
6.explanation: The table `cats` does not exist in the database.

7.name: Invalid schema name
7.description: The query references a schema that does not exist or is not defined.
7.explanation: The schema `shop` does not exist in the database. In this example, the customer table resides in the `public` schema.

8.name: Misspellings
8.description: The query contains misspelled table names, column names, or other identifiers.
8.explanation: The table `costumer` does not exist in the database. It is a misspelling of `customer`.

9.name: Synonyms
9.description: The query uses synonyms or alternative names for tables or columns that do not exist.
9.explanation: The table `clients` does not exist in this schema. It is used as a synonym for `customer`, which is the correct table name.

10.name: Omitting quotes around character data
10.description: Character data values are not enclosed in quotes, which is required for string literals.
10.explanation: Character data values must be enclosed in single quotes (`'`) to be recognized as string literals.

11.name: Failure to specify column name twice
11.description: When using multiple conditions on the same column, the column name is not specified for each condition.
11.explanation: Each condition in the WHERE clause must explicitly specify the column cName it applies to.
11.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND age < 25;

12.name: IS where not applicable
12.description: The `IS` operator is used for comparisons on values other than `NULL` or boolean expressions.
12.explanation: The IS operator should only be used for checking `NULL` values or boolean expressions (e.g., `IS TRUE`, `IS FALSE`). For other comparisons, use standard comparison operators like `=`, `<>`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`.

13.name: Data type mismatch
13.description: The query uses incompatible data types in an operation or comparison.
13.explanation: The condition in the WHERE clause compares a string column (cName) with an integer value (7), which is not valid.

14.name: Using aggregate function outside SELECT or HAVING
14.description: An aggregate function is used in a part of the query where it is not allowed, such as `WHERE` or `GROUP BY`.
14.explanation: Aggregate functions can only be used in the `SELECT` clause, or the `HAVING` clause when grouping results. This query is attempting to use `AVG` in the `WHERE` clause, which is not allowed.
14.correction:
SELECT city
FROM customer
GROUP BY city
HAVING AVG(age) > 18;

15.name: Aggregate functions cannot be nested
15.description: Aggregate functions cannot be nested within each other in a query.
15.explanation: This query attempts to nest the AVG function inside the SUM function, which is not allowed. Instead, you can use a subquery to first calculate the average scores per group and then sum those averages.

16.name: Extraneous or omitted grouping column
16.description: When using GROUP BY, all non-aggregated columns in the SELECT clause must be included in the GROUP BY clause. Only aggregated columns or columns included in the GROUP BY clause can be referenced in the HAVING clause.
16.explanation: The query selects the column `cName` which is neither aggregated nor included in the `GROUP BY` clause. This leads to ambiguity about which `cName` to return for each group. To fix this, either include `cName` in the`GROUP BY` clause or remove it from the `SELECT` clause.

17.name: Strange HAVING: HAVING without GROUP BY
17.description: The query uses a `HAVING` clause without a corresponding `GROUP BY` clause.
17.explanation: The `HAVING` clause is used to filter grouped results and should always be used in conjunction with a `GROUP BY` clause.
17.correction:
SELECT city, AVG(age)
FROM customer
GROUP BY city
HAVING AVG(age) > 18;

18.name: Too many columns in subquery
18.description: A subquery returns more columns than expected in the context where it is used.
18.example:
SELECT *
FROM inventory
WHERE sID IN (
    SELECT *
    FROM store
    WHERE sName = 'Coop'
);
18.explanation: The subquery in the WHERE clause must return exactly one column to compare with `inventory.sID`. Returning all store columns causes a column-count mismatch.
18.correction:
```sql
SELECT *
FROM inventory
WHERE sID IN (
    SELECT sID
    FROM store
    WHERE sName = 'Coop'
);```

19.name: Missing quantifier
19.description: A comparison is performed with a subquery that can possibly return multiple values, regardless of the data currently present in the database.
19.example:
SELECT *
FROM inventory
WHERE unit_price >= (
    SELECT unit_price
    FROM product
    NATURAL JOIN inventory
    WHERE pName = 'Banana'
);
19.explanation: The value of `unit_price` is directly compared to the result of the subquery.  This works fine only if the subquery returns a single value (i.e. the `product` table contains only one entry whose cName is *'Banana'*). However, if the table is later updated, and a new product with the same cName is added, the query becomes invalid.  This problem can be solved by using a quantifier in the comparison (ANY/ALL).
19.correction:
SELECT *
FROM inventory
WHERE unit_price >= ANY(
    SELECT unit_price
    FROM product
    NATURAL JOIN inventory
    WHERE pName = 'Banana'
);

20.name: Confusing function with function parameter
20.description: A function is confused with its parameter, leading to incorrect query syntax or logic.
20.explanation: The query incorrectly uses parentheses around the function name COUNT, which makes it look like a parameter rather than a function call. The correct syntax is to use COUNT without parentheses around it.

21.name: Using WHERE twice
21.description: The query contains multiple `WHERE` clauses, which is not allowed.
21.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE age > 18
WHERE city = 'Genoa';
21.explanation: A query can only have one `WHERE` clause. Combine multiple conditions using logical operators like `AND` or `OR`.
21.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND city = 'Genoa';

22.name: Omitting the FROM clause
22.description: The query is missing the `FROM` clause and does not select a constant value.
22.explanation: The `FROM` clause is required in a SELECT statement to specify the table from which to retrieve data.

23.name: Comparison with NULL
23.description: The query uses a comparison with NULL using operators like `=` or `!=` instead of `IS NULL` or `IS NOT NULL`.
23.explanation: When checking for NULL values, use `IS NULL` or `IS NOT NULL` instead of standard comparison operators.

24.name: Omitting the semicolon
24.description: The query is missing the terminating semicolon at the end of the statement.
24.explanation: SQL standard requires each statement to end with a semicolon (`;`). Make sure to include it at the end of your queries.

25.name: Date time field overflow
25.description: An invalid date or time value is used in the query.
25.explanation: The date '2024-02-33' is invalid because a month cannot have 33 days.

26.name: Duplicate clause
26.description: A clause is repeated in the query, such as multiple `WHERE` or `ORDER BY` clauses.
26.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
ORDER BY cID;
26.explanation: This query is invalid because it contains two `ORDER BY` clauses. Each clause should only appear once in a query.

27.name: Using an undefined correlation name
27.description: The query references a correlation name (alias) that has not been defined.
27.example:
SELECT c2.cName
FROM customer AS c;
27.explanation: This query renames the customer table to `c` but then tries to reference it as `c2`, which is not defined.
27.correction:
SELECT c.cName
FROM customer AS c;

28.name: Confusing table names with column names
28.description: A table name is used where a column name is expected, or vice versa.
28.explanation: This query tries to access a column called `customer` from the `city` table, which does not exist. The correct syntax is to specify the table name followed by the column name.

29.name: Confusing the order of keywords (e.g., FROM customer SELECT fee)
29.description: Keywords in the SQL statement are in the wrong order.
29.explanation: Ensure that SQL keywords are in the correct order: `SELECT`, `FROM`, `WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT`, `OFFSET`.

30.name: Confusing the syntax of keywords (e.g., LIKE ('A,' 'B'))
30.description: Each SQL keyword has a specific syntax that must be followed.
30.explanation: The BETWEEN operator requires two values to define the range: a lower bound and an upper bound.

31.name: Omitting commas
31.description: When listing multiple columns or values, commas are missing between them.
31.example:
SELECT cID city street
FROM customer;
31.explanation: This query is missing commas between the column names in the `SELECT` clause. Ensure that each column name is separated by a comma.
31.correction:
SELECT cID, city, street
FROM customer;

32.name: Unmatched brackets
32.description: Curly and square brackets are used instead of parentheses, or parentheses are unmatched.
32.explanation: All opening parentheses must have a corresponding closing parenthesis.

33.name: Curly or square brackets
33.description: Curly and square brackets are used instead of parentheses.
33.explanation: Only parentheses ( "`(`" and "`)`" ) are allowed for grouping expressions in SQL.

34.name: Nonstandard keywords or standard keywords in wrong context
34.description: The query uses invalid keywords or standard keywords in inappropriate contexts.
34.explanation: The correct keyword for sorting results in SQL is `ORDER BY`, not `SORT BY`.

35.name: Nonstandard operators (e.g., &&, \|\| or ==)
35.description: The query uses operators that are not part of the standard SQL syntax, such as `&&`, `||`, or `==` instead of `AND`, `OR`, or `=`.
35.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    cID == 18
    && city == 'A';
35.explanation: The operators `==` and `&&`, although common in programming languages, are not valid in SQL. Use `=` and `AND` instead.
35.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    cID = 18
    AND city = 'A';

36.name: Additional semicolon
36.description: An extra semicolon is present in the query, which can lead to syntax errors.
36.explanation: Ensure that there is only one semicolon at the end of the SQL statement.

37.name: Different tuples in set operation
37.description: Two queries combined with a set operation (`UNION`/`INTERSECT`/`EXCEPT`) return tuples with different cardinalities.
37.example:
SELECT city
FROM customer
UNION
SELECT city, street
FROM store;
37.explanation: The former SELECT returns a single column (`city`), while the latter returns two columns (`city` and `street`). Set operations require both results to have the same number of columns.
37.correction:
SELECT city
FROM customer
UNION
SELECT city
FROM store;

38.name: Implied, tautological or inconsistent expression
38.description: Query contains expressions that are always true, always false, or logically redundant.
38.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 30
    AND age > 25
    AND cName = cName;
38.explanation: The expression `age > 30 AND age > 25` is redundant because if `age` is greater than 30, it is inherently greater than 25. Additionally, the condition `cName = cName` is always true for all rows.  Simplifying the query to remove these redundancies will improve clarity and performance, without altering its logic.

39.name: DISTINCT in SUM or AVG
39.description: Using `DISTINCT` within `SUM` or `AVG` means that only unique values are summed or averaged, which is almost never the intended behavior.
39.explanation: This query first considers all the unique customer ages (each value is counted only once) and then calculates the average of those unique ages. If there are 200 customer aged 20 and 1 customer aged 30, the average will be `(20 + 30) / 2 = 25`, which does not accurately reflect the average age of all customers.

40.name: DISTINCT that might remove important duplicates
40.description: Using `DISTINCT` removes duplicate rows from the result set, which may inadvertently exclude important data.
40.explanation: If multiple customer share the same name and live in Genoa, using DISTINCT will return only one instance of that name, potentially omitting other customers with the same name who also live in the same city. This could lead to incomplete or misleading results.

41.name: Mixing a >0 with IS NOT NULL or empty string with NULL
41.description: Using `> 0` or `= ''` to check for non-null values instead of `IS NOT NULL` or `IS NULL`.
41.explanation: This query erroneously attempts to find customers for whom we don't know in which street they live, by checking for an empty string in the `street` column. However, this query actually selects customers who live in a street named `''` *(empty string)*.

42.name: NULL in IN/ANY/ALL subquery
42.description: Using nullable values in `IN`, `ANY`, or `ALL` subqueries can lead to unexpected results due to the way NULLs are handled in SQL.
42.explanation: This query attempts to find customers whose city appears in the `store` table. However, if the subquery returns `NULL` values, the `IN` condition can evaluate to `UNKNOWN`, causing matching rows to be excluded unexpectedly.
42.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE city IN (
    SELECT city
    FROM store
    WHERE city IS NOT NULL
);

43.name: Join condition on unmatchable column
43.description: A join condition is performed on columns that cannot logically match, resulting in an empty result set.
43.example:
SELECT *
FROM
    store s
    JOIN inventory i ON s.sID = i.unit_price;
43.explanation: This query attempts to join the `store` and `inventory` tables based on the condition that a stores's `sID` equals the inventory's `unit_price`. Since `sID` and `unit_price` are fundamentally different types of data, this join condition is illogical and will likely result in no matching rows.
43.correction:
SELECT *
FROM
    store s
    JOIN inventory i ON s.sID = i.sID;

44.name: Many duplicates
44.description: The query returns many times the same values, making the results difficult to interpret.
44.explanation: This query aims to find all the cities where customers live. However, if multiple customers live in the same city, that city will appear multiple times in the result set, leading to redundancy and making it harder to analyze the data.

45.name: Constant column output
45.description: A column in the output has the same constant value for all rows.
45.explanation: The `city` column does not provide any useful information because it has the same value (*'Genoa'*) for all customer in the result set. This redundancy can clutter the output and make it harder to focus on relevant data.

46.name: Duplicate column output
46.description: Multiple columns in the output always contain identical values.
46.example:
SELECT cID, cName, cName
FROM customer;
46.explanation: The query selects the `cName` column twice, resulting in duplicate data in the output. This redundancy can confuse users and make it harder to interpret the results.

47.name: AND instead of OR
47.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND city = 'Genoa';
47.explanation: This query finds customers older than 18 who live in Genoa. However, this does not match the data demand. In this case, `OR` should have been used instead of `AND`.
47.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE 
    age > 18
    OR city = 'Genoa';

48.name: OR instead of AND
48.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    OR city = 'Genoa';
48.explanation: This query finds customers who are older than 18, as well as those who live in Genoa, regardless of their age. However, this does not match the data demand. In this case, `AND` should have been used instead of `OR`.
48.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND city = 'Genoa';

49.name: Extraneous NOT operator
49.description: A logical condition uses `NOT` where it is not needed, leading to incorrect results.
49.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND NOT city = 'Genoa';
49.explanation: This query is intended to find customers who are older than 18 and live in Genoa. However, the `NOT` operator negates the city condition, resulting in customers who are older than 18 but do not live in Genoa.
49.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND city = 'Genoa';

50.name: Missing NOT operator
50.description: A logical condition is missing a `NOT` operator, leading to incorrect results.
50.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND city = 'Genoa';
50.explanation: This query is intended to find customers who are older than 18 and do not live in Genoa. However, without the `NOT` operator, it returns customers who are older than 18 and live in Genoa.
50.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND NOT city = 'Genoa';

51.name: Substituting existence negation with <>
51.description: The query uses `<>` to check for non-existence instead of `NOT IN` or `NOT EXISTS`.
51.example:
SELECT *
FROM store s
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM inventory i
    WHERE
        i.sID = s.sID
        AND i.unit_price <> 5
);
51.explanation: This query uses `<>` to check for inventory items that do not cost 5, but it does not correctly enforce the condition that there should be no inventory items that cost 5. Instead, it checks if there exists at least one inventory item that does not cost 5, which is not the intended logic. The correct approach is to use `NOT EXISTS` to ensure that there are no inventory items with a unit price of 5 for each store.
51.correction:
SELECT *
FROM store s
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM inventory i
    WHERE
        i.sID = s.sID
        AND i.unit_price = 5
);

52.name: Incorrect comparison operator or incorrect value compared
52.description: A condition uses the wrong comparison operator or compares against an incorrect value, leading to unintended results.
52.explanation: The query uses the `<` operator instead of `>`, which results in listing customers who are younger than 18 instead of those who are older.

53.name: Incorrect table reference
53.description: The query references the wrong table, leading to incorrect or unexpected results.
53.explanation: The query references the `store` table instead of the `customer` table, which is where the city information for customers is stored. As a result, it retrieves cities from the `store` table rather than from the `customer` table, which does not meet the data demand.

54.name: Missing table reference
54.description: The query references fewer tables than necessary, leading to incomplete results.
54.explanation: The query only references the `store` table, which does not contain information about inventory. As a result, it fails to identify which stores have items in inventory, leading to incomplete results.
54.correction:
SELECT sID
FROM
    store
    NATURAL JOIN inventory;

55.name: Extraneous table reference
55.description: The query references more tables than necessary, leading to incorrect or unexpected results.
55.explanation: The query references both the `customer` and `store` tables, but the data demand only requires information from the `customer` table. The inclusion of the `store` table creates a Cartesian product, resulting in multiple rows for each customer name, which is not the intended outcome.

56.name: Join condition on incorrect column
56.description: A join condition uses the wrong column, leading to incorrect or unexpected results.
56.explanation: The query attempts to join the `store` and `inventory` tables using the condition `s.sID = i.pID`. However, `sID` is the store ID and `pID` is the product ID, so this condition does not correctly relate the two tables. As a result, the query will not return the intended results of listing stores along with their inventory.

57.name: Join condition with incorrect comparison operator
57.description: A join condition uses the wrong comparison operator, leading to incorrect or unexpected results.
57.explanation: The query attempts to find pairs of customers who live in the same city, but it uses the `<>` operator, which means "not equal". As a result, it retrieves pairs of customers who live in different cities instead of those who live in the same city.

58.name: Omitting a join condition
58.description: A query references multiple tables but does not include the necessary join conditions to connect them, leading to incomplete or incorrect results.
58.example:
SELECT *
FROM
    store,
    inventory
WHERE
    city = 'Genoa';
58.explanation: This query does not include any join conditions to connect the `store` and `inventory` tables. As a result, it produces a Cartesian product of the two tables filtered only by the city condition on the `store` table. This means that it will return all combinations of stores in Genoa with all inventory items, regardless of whether those items are actually in those stores.
58.correction:
SELECT *
FROM
    store
    NATURAL JOIN inventory
WHERE
    city = 'Genoa';

59.name: Condition on OUTER JOIN
59.description: Using a JOIN ON condition on the left table of a LEFT OUTER JOIN affects only the right table, which can lead to unexpected results.
59.example:
SELECT *
FROM
    customer c
    LEFT OUTER JOIN store s ON s.city = c.city AND s.sName = 'Coop';
59.explanation: The condition `s.sName = 'Coop'` is applied in the `ON` clause and affects how rows match on the right side. If a store's name does not satisfy the condition, the row can still appear with `NULL` values, which is not the intended behavior. To filter rows, the condition should be placed in the `WHERE` clause.
59.correction:
SELECT *
FROM
    customer c
    LEFT OUTER JOIN store s ON s.city = c.city
WHERE s.sName = 'Coop';

60.name: Improper nesting of expressions
60.description: Expressions are not properly nested with parentheses, leading to incorrect evaluation order.
60.explanation: In this query, the multiplication operator has a higher precedence than the addition operator. As a result, `5 * 2` is evaluated first, giving 10, and then `age + 10` is evaluated. This is not the intended calculation. The correct evaluation should be `(age + 5) * 2`, which requires proper nesting with parentheses.

61.name: Improper nesting of subqueries
61.description: Subqueries are nested improperly, leading to a different evaluation order than intended.
61.example:
SELECT *
FROM store s
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM inventory i
  WHERE i.sID = s.sID
    AND EXISTS (
      SELECT 1
      FROM product p
      WHERE p.pID = i.pID
        AND p.suffix = 'XL'
    )
);
61.explanation: This query is intended to enforce two separate conditions: the store has inventory rows, and none of those rows refers to a product with suffix `XL`. Nesting `EXISTS` inside `NOT EXISTS` leads to selecting stores that do not have any inventory rows at all, which is not the intended outcome. The correct approach is to use two separate `EXISTS` conditions at the same level.
61.correction:
SELECT *
FROM store s
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM inventory i
  WHERE i.sID = s.sID
)
AND NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM inventory i
  JOIN product p ON p.pID = i.pID
  WHERE i.sID = s.sID
    AND p.suffix = 'XL'
);

62.name: Extraneous quotes
62.description: A column name is enclosed in quotes, effectively turning it into a string literal.
62.explanation: In this query, the condition `c.city = 's.city'` compares the `city` column of the `customer` table to the string literal `'s.city'`, which is not correct. To compare the `city` column of both tables, the quotes around `s.city` must be removed.

63.name: Missing expression
63.description: A logical expression required by the data demand is missing from the query.
63.explanation: The query only checks for customers older than 18 but does not filter for customers living in Genoa.
63.correction:
SELECT cName
FROM customer
WHERE 
    age > 18
    AND city = 'Genoa';

64.name: Extraneous expression
64.description: A logical expression not required by the data demand is present in the query.
64.example:
SELECT sID
FROM store
WHERE
    sName = 'Coop'
    AND city = 'Genoa';
64.explanation: The query lists all Coop stores located in Genoa, which is not what the data demand required. The extraneous condition `city = 'Genoa'` should be removed.

65.name: Expression on incorrect column
65.description: A logical expression required by the data demand is present in the query, but on the wrong column.
65.explanation: The condition `= 'Coop'` is correct, but it should be applied on the `sName` column, instead of the `street` column.

66.name: Expression in incorrect clause
66.description: A correct logical expression is present in the WRONG clause (i.e. `WHERE` instead of `HAVING`, or vice versa).
66.example:
SELECT city, AVG(age)
FROM customer
WHERE age < 30
GROUP BY city;
66.explanation: This query answers the following data demand: *List the average age of customers for each city. Consider only customers younger than 30.*  To correctly satisfy the data demand, the condition `age < 30` must be moved to the `HAVING` clause, since it must apply on the grouping result instead of individual rows.
66.correction:
SELECT city, AVG(age)
FROM customer
GROUP BY city
HAVING AVG(age) < 30;

67.name: Wildcards without LIKE
67.description: Wildcards such as % or _ are used with `=` instead of `LIKE`, resulting in a literal comparison rather than a pattern match.
67.explanation: This query selects all customer whose name is exactly `J%n`, including the percent sign, rather than matching names that start with 'J' and end with 'n'.

68.name: Wrong wildcard
68.description: The wrong wildcard is used in a `LIKE` pattern. For example, using `%` instead of `_` to match a single character, or vice versa.
68.explanation: This query uses the `%` wildcard, which matches any sequence of characters (including zero characters), instead of the `_` wildcard, which matches exactly one character. As a result, this query will return customers whose names start with 'J' and end with 'n', regardless of how many characters are in between, which does not satisfy the data demand.

69.name: Invalid wildcard
69.description: Invalid wildcard characters are used in pattern matching instead of the standard SQL wildcards, such as using * instead of % or using ? instead of _.
69.explanation: The query uses the `*` wildcard to match any sequence of characters, which is common in other programming languages but not in SQL. In SQL, the `%` wildcard is used for pattern matching. As a result, the query does not return any results because it is looking for names that literally contain 'J*n' instead of names that start with 'J' and end with 'n'.

70.name: Extraneous column in SELECT
70.description: A column is included in the `SELECT` clause that is not expected or needed for the intended result.
70.example:
SELECT cID, cName, street
FROM customer;
70.explanation: The data demand requires only the IDs and names of customers, but the query also includes the `street` column in the SELECT clause. This adds unnecessary data to the result set.

71.name: Missing column from SELECT
71.description: A required column is missing from the `SELECT` clause.
71.explanation: The exercise requires both the names and ages of customer, but the query only selects the former.

72.name: Missing DISTINCT from SELECT
72.description: A `DISTINCT` keyword is missing from the `SELECT` clause, leading to duplicate rows in the result set where uniqueness is required by the exercise.
72.explanation: The query retrieves the cities of all customers, but it does not use `DISTINCT`, which means that if multiple customers live in the same city, that city will appear multiple times in the result set. This does not meet the data demand for listing unique cities.

73.name: Missing AS from SELECT
73.description: A column presents a different name from what is required by the exercise, due to the absence of an `AS` alias.
73.explanation: The query retrieves the names of all customers, but it does not label the output column as `name`, which is required by the data demand.
73.correction:
SELECT cName AS name
FROM customer;

74.name: Missing column from ORDER BY clause
74.description: A required column is missing from the `ORDER BY` clause.
74.explanation: The query retrieves the names and ages of all customers but only orders the results by `cName`.  Customers with the same name are unsorted within their group.

75.name: Incorrect column in ORDER BY clause
75.description: An incorrect column is used in the `ORDER BY` clause, leading to results being sorted in an unintended manner.
75.explanation: The query orders the results by the `city` column instead of the `cName` column, which is not what the data demand specifies. This results in customers being sorted by their city rather than their names.

76.name: Incorrect ordering of rows
76.description: The `ORDER BY` clause sorts the results in the wrong order (ascending instead of descending, or vice versa).
76.explanation: The exercise requires the results to be ordered by `cName` in descending order, but the query sorts the results in ascending order instead.

77.name: Missing WHERE clause
77.description: A `WHERE` clause is missing from the query when the data demand requires filtering of results based on specific conditions.
77.explanation: The query does not satisfy its data demand, since it does not include a `WHERE` clause to filter the results based on the specified conditions of age and city. As a result, it returns the names of all customers, regardless of their age or city of residence, which is not what the data demand specifies.
77.correction:
SELECT cName
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 18
    AND city = 'Genoa';

78.name: Missing GROUP BY clause
78.description: A `GROUP BY` clause is missing from the query when the data demand requires aggregation of results based on one or more columns.
78.explanation: The query calculates the average age of all customers but does not group the results by city. As a result, it returns a single average age for all customers, not for each city.

79.name: Missing HAVING clause
79.description: A `HAVING` clause is missing from the query when the data demand requires filtering of grouped results based on an aggregate condition.
79.explanation: The query calculates the average age of customers for each city but does not include a `HAVING` clause to filter the results based on the condition that the average age should be greater than 30. As a result, it returns the average age for all cities, not just those where the average age exceeds 30.
79.correction:
SELECT city, AVG(age)
FROM customer
GROUP BY city
HAVING AVG(age) > 30;

80.name: Missing ORDER BY clause
80.description: An `ORDER BY` clause is missing from the query when the data demand requires the results to be sorted in a specific order.
80.explanation: The query does not satisfy its data demand, since it does not include an `ORDER BY` clause to sort the results in alphabetical order by customer name.

81.name: Missing LIMIT clause
81.description: The `LIMIT` clause is missing from the query when the data demand requires limiting the number of rows returned in the result set.
81.explanation: The query retrieves all customers from the database, but it does not include a `LIMIT` clause to restrict the number of rows returned to 10, as required by the data demand.

82.name: Missing OFFSET clause
82.description: The `OFFSET` clause is missing from the query when the data demand requires skipping a certain number of rows before starting to return results.
82.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
LIMIT 3;
82.explanation: The query doesn't satisfy its data demand, since it lists the first three customers instead of the second, third and fourth customers. The `OFFSET` clause is needed to skip the first customer.
82.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
LIMIT 3
OFFSET 1;

83.name: Extraneous WHERE clause
83.description: A `WHERE` clause is included in the query when the data demand does not require any filtering of results.
83.explanation: The data demand is to list the names of all customers, regardless of their city. The inclusion of the `WHERE` clause filters the results to only include customers from Genoa, which is not what the data demand specifies. This results in an incomplete list of customer names.

84.name: Extraneous GROUP BY clause
84.description: A `GROUP BY` clause is included in the query when the data demand does not require any aggregation or grouping of the results.
84.explanation: The query groups customers by city and calculates the average age for each city. However, the data demand is to list the average age of all customers, not grouped by city.

85.name: Extraneous HAVING clause
85.description: A `HAVING` clause is included in the query when the data demand does not require any filtering of grouped results.
85.example:
SELECT city, AVG(age)
FROM customer
GROUP BY city
HAVING AVG(age) > 30;
85.explanation: The query uses a `HAVING` clause to filter groups of customers based on their average age. However, the data demand is to list the average age of all customers, not to filter groups. The `HAVING` clause is extraneous in this context.

86.name: Extraneous ORDER BY clause
86.description: An `ORDER BY` clause is included in the query when the data demand does not require any specific ordering of the results.
86.explanation: The data demand does not specify any ordering for the results, so including an `ORDER BY` clause is unnecessary and adds complexity to the query.

87.name: Extraneous LIMIT clause
87.description: The `LIMIT` clause is included in the query when the data demand does not require restricting the number of results returned.
87.explanation: The query doesn't satisfy its data demand, since it lists only 10 customers instead of all those present in the database.

88.name: Extraneous OFFSET clause
88.description: The `OFFSET` clause is included in the query when the data demand does not require skipping any rows in the result set.
88.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
LIMIT 3
OFFSET 1;
88.explanation: The query doesn't satisfy its data demand, since it lists customers the second, third and fourth customers, instead of the first three.
88.correction:
```sql
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
LIMIT 3;

89.name: Incorrect LIMIT
89.description: The `LIMIT` clause is used with an incorrect value.
89.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
LIMIT 10;
89.explanation: The query doesn't satisfy its data demand, since it lists the first 10 customers instead of the first 3 customers.
89.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
LIMIT 3;

90.name: Incorrect OFFSET
90.description: The `OFFSET` clause is used with an incorrect value.
90.example:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
OFFSET 10;
90.explanation: The query uses an incorrect offset value of 10, which skips more customers than intended. The data demand requires skipping only the first 5 customers.
90.correction:
SELECT *
FROM customer
ORDER BY cName
OFFSET 5;

91.name: Incorrect function
91.description: An incorrect function is used for the intended operation, leading to unexpected results.
91.explanation: The exercise requires calculating the average age of customers, but the query uses the `SUM` function instead of `AVG`, resulting in the total age rather than the average age.

92.name: DISTINCT as function parameter where not applicable
92.description: DISTINCT is used within a function where it is not appropriate or necessary.
92.explanation: `DISTINCT` causes `COUNT` to count unique ages only. Multiple customers with the same age are counted as one.

93.name: Missing DISTINCT from function parameter
93.description: `DISTINCT` is missing within a function where it is required to ensure repeated values are not counted multiple times.
93.explanation: The query counts all entries in the `city` column, including duplicates. If multiple customers are from the same city, they will be counted multiple times, which does not meet the data demand for counting unique cities.

94.name: Incorrect column as function parameter
94.description: An incorrect column is used as a parameter for a function, leading to unintended results.
94.explanation: The query calculates the average of the `quantity` column instead of the `unit_price` column, which is not what the data demand specifies. This results in an incorrect calculation of the average unit price.

95.name: Unnecessary complication
95.description: The query is more complex than necessary to achieve the intended result.  This is an umbrella category for various types of unnecessary complications in SQL queries that do not fit into more specific categories.
95.example:
SELECT cName
FROM customer
WHERE
    age > 36 / 2
    OR age = 9 * (12 - 2);
95.explanation: The exercise requires selecting customer at least 18 years old. However, the query uses unnecessary arithmetic operations to express the age condition, making it more complicated than needed.

96.name: Unnecessary DISTINCT in SELECT clause
96.description: `DISTINCT` is used on a `SELECT` clause where duplicate values cannot occur, adding unnecessary complexity.
96.example:
SELECT DISTINCT cID, cName
FROM customer;
96.explanation: The `cID` column is a primary key, meaning each value is unique. Therefore, using `DISTINCT` is redundant since there cannot be any duplicate rows in the result set.

97.name: Unnecessary table reference
97.description: A table is joined just to access a value which is already available as a foreign key.
97.explanation: In this query, the `product` table is joined to access the `pID` column, which is already available in the `inventory` table as a foreign key. The join is unnecessary and can be omitted without affecting the result of the query.
97.correction:
SELECT i.sID, i.pID
FROM inventory i;

98.name: Unused correlation name
98.description: A correlation name (alias) is defined for a table but not used in the query.
98.example:
SELECT cName
FROM customer AS c;
98.explanation: The query defines a correlation name `c` for the `customer` table but does not use it anywhere in the query. This adds unnecessary complexity without any benefit.

99.name: Tables have the same data
99.description: The query references two tables that always contain the same data.
99.example:
SELECT *
FROM
    customer a
    JOIN customer b ON a.cID = b.cID;
99.explanation: The query joins the customer table with itself on the `cID` column, which is a primary key. Since each `cID` is unique, the join will always pair each customer with themselves, resulting in identical data from both tables. This redundancy adds unnecessary complexity without any benefit.

100.name: Correlation name identical to table name
100.description: The query defines a correlation name (alias) for a table that is the same as the original table name, making the alias redundant.
100.explanation: The query defines a correlation name (alias) `customer` for the `customer` table, which is identical to the original table name. This redundancy adds unnecessary complexity without any benefit.

101.name: Unnecessarily general comparison operator
101.description: An operator used for a comparison can be replaced by a more specific one without changing the logic.
101.explanation: The query is intended to find the oldest customer. However, the `>=` part of the operator is unnecessary because no customer can be older than the maximum age. Using the more specific `=` operator makes the query clearer and more efficient.

102.name: LIKE without wildcards
102.description: The `LIKE` operator is used without any wildcard characters, making it equivalent to a simple equality comparison.
102.explanation: The query uses the `LIKE` operator to compare the `cName` column to the string 'John' without any wildcard characters (such as `%` or `_`). This means that the `LIKE` operator is functioning as a simple equality comparison, and there is no need for it in this context.

103.name: Unnecessarily complicated SELECT in EXISTS subquery
103.description: The `SELECT` clause in an `EXISTS` subquery returns more than a single column, which is unnecessary since `EXISTS` only checks for the existence of rows.
103.example:
SELECT cName
FROM customer c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT s.sID, s.sName
    FROM store s
    WHERE c.cName = s.sName
);
103.explanation: The `EXISTS` subquery is intended to find customers who have the same name as a store. However, the `SELECT` clause returns multiple store columns, which is unnecessary because `EXISTS` only checks whether at least one row is returned.
103.correction:
SELECT cName
FROM customer c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM store s
    WHERE c.cName = s.sName
);

104.name: IN/EXISTS can be replaced by comparison
104.description: A subuery using `IN` or `EXISTS` can be simplified to a direct comparison.
104.explanation: The query is intended to find customers who are younger than 18. However, it uses a subquery with `IN`, which can be simplified to a direct comparison. Since `cID` is a unique identifier, we can directly compare the age without needing a subquery.

105.name: Unnecessary aggregate function
105.description: An aggregate function is used on a single value.
105.example: SELECT MAX(age) FROM customer WHERE cID = 123;
105.explanation: The query retrieves the age of a specific customer with `cID = 123`. Since the `WHERE` clause filters the results to a single row, using the `MAX` aggregate function is unnecessary, as there will only be one value to return.

106.name: Unnecessary DISTINCT in aggregate function
106.description: `DISTINCT` is used within an aggregate function where all values are already unique, making it redundant.
106.explanation: The `cID` column is a primary key, meaning each value is unique. Therefore, using `DISTINCT` within the `COUNT` function doesn't change the result, as there cannot be any duplicate ids to count.

107.name: Unnecessary argument of COUNT
107.description: `COUNT` is used with a specific column name instead of using `COUNT(*)` or `COUNT(1)` when counting non-nullable columns.
107.explanation: The `cID` column is the primary key of the `customer` table and as such it cannot contain any `NULL` values. Therefore, using `COUNT(cID)` is equivalent to `COUNT(*)`, as all rows will be counted. Using `COUNT(*)` is more straightforward and efficient in this case.

108.name: Unnecessary GROUP BY in EXISTS subquery
108.description: A `GROUP BY` clause is used in an `EXISTS` subquery where it is not needed, as `EXISTS` only checks for the existence of rows.
108.example:
SELECT cName
FROM customer c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM store s
    WHERE c.cName = s.sName
    GROUP BY s.city
);
108.explanation: The `GROUP BY` clause in the `EXISTS` subquery is unnecessary because `EXISTS` only checks whether at least one row is returned by the subquery. The grouping does not affect the existence check and adds unnecessary complexity to the query.
108.correction:
SELECT cName
FROM customer c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM store s
    WHERE c.cName = s.sName
);

109.name: GROUP BY with singleton groups
109.description: `GROUP BY` is used on groups that each contain only a single row, making the grouping unnecessary.
109.explanation: The query groups customers by their unique identifier `cID`, which means that each group will contain only one customer. Since there is only one row in each group, the `GROUP BY` clause does not serve any purpose, and the same result can be achieved without grouping.

110.name: GROUP BY with only a single group
110.description: `GROUP BY` is used in a way that results in only a single group, making the grouping unnecessary.
110.example:
SELECT AVG(age)
FROM customer
WHERE city = 'Genoa'
GROUP BY city;
110.explanation: The query groups customers by city, but the `WHERE` clause filters the results to only include customers from Genoa. This means that there will be only one group (Genoa) in the result, making the `GROUP BY` clause unnecessary. The same result can be achieved without grouping, as we are only interested in the average age of customers from Genoa.

111.name: GROUP BY can be replaced with DISTINCT
111.description: All `GROUP BY` columns are also in the `SELECT` clause without any aggregate functions, making `DISTINCT` a simpler alternative.
111.explanation: The query groups customers by `city` and `street`, but it does not perform any aggregation. Since all `GROUP BY` columns are also in the `SELECT` clause without any aggregate functions, the same result can be achieved using `DISTINCT`, which is simpler and more efficient in this case.
111.correction: SELECT DISTINCT cName, cID FROM customer;

112.name: UNION can be replaced by OR
112.description: Two `SELECT` statements combined with `UNION` select the same data from the same table and have mutually exclusive conditions. In such cases, the `UNION` can be replaced by a single `SELECT` statement with an `OR` condition.
112.example:
SELECT cID, cName
FROM customer
WHERE age < 18
UNION
SELECT cID, cName
FROM customer
WHERE age > 30;
112.explanation: The two queries retrieve names of customer from disjoint age groups (younger than 18 and older than 30). Since there is no overlap between these two groups, the same result can be achieved with a single query using an `OR` condition, which is more efficient and easier to read.
112.correction:
SELECT cID, cName
FROM customer
WHERE
    age < 18
    OR age > 30;

113.name: Unnecessary column in ORDER BY clause
113.description: A column is included in the `ORDER BY` clause that does not affect the ordering of the results due to functional dependencies.
113.explanation: The query orders the results by both `cID` and `cName`. However, since `cID` is a primary key, it uniquely identifies each row. Therefore, ordering by `cName` after `cID` does not change the order of the results, making it unnecessary.

114.name: ORDER BY in subquery
114.description: An `ORDER BY` clause is used in a subquery where it does not affect the final result set.
114.example:
SELECT city
FROM customer
WHERE city IN (
    SELECT city
    FROM store
    ORDER BY city
);
114.explanation: The main query retrieves cities from the `customer` table that are present in the list of cities returned by the subquery. The order of cities in the subquery's result does not affect which cities are included in the final output, making the `ORDER BY` clause unnecessary in this context.

115.name: Inefficient HAVING
115.description: A condition in the `HAVING` clause does not depend on aggregate functions and can be moved to the `WHERE` clause.
115.example:
SELECT age, COUNT(*)
FROM customer
GROUP BY age
HAVING age IS NOT NULL;
115.explanation: The HAVING clause is used to filter groups based on aggregate conditions. However, the condition `age IS NOT NULL` does not depend on aggregation and can be applied before grouping, which is more efficient.
115.correction:
SELECT age, COUNT(*)
FROM customer
WHERE age IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY age;

116.name: Inefficient UNION
116.description: A `UNION` operation can be replaced by `UNION ALL` without changing the result set, improving performance.
116.example:
SELECT city 
FROM customer
WHERE city < 'M'
UNION
SELECT city
FROM store
WHERE city > 'M';
116.explanation: The query combines cities from the `customer` and `store` tables using `UNION`, which eliminates duplicate rows. However, the conditions in the `WHERE` clauses ensure that there will be no overlapping cities between the two sets (cities less than 'M' from `customer` and cities greater than 'M' from `store`). Therefore, there are no duplicates to eliminate, and using `UNION ALL` would yield the same result more efficiently.
116.correction:
SELECT city 
FROM customer
WHERE city < 'M'
UNION ALL
SELECT city
FROM store
WHERE city > 'M';

117.name: Condition in the subquery can be moved up
117.description: A condition in a subquery references only values from the outer query and can be moved to the outer clause.
117.example:
SELECT cName
FROM customer c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM store s
    WHERE
        s.sName = c.cName
        AND c.age > 18
    );
117.explanation: The condition `c.age > 18` in the subquery only references the outer query's table `customer`. Therefore, it can be moved to the outer `WHERE` clause, which can improve query readability and potentially performance.
117.correction:
SELECT cName
FROM customer c
WHERE
    c.age > 18
    AND EXISTS (
        SELECT 1
        FROM store s
        WHERE s.sName = c.cName
    );

118.name: OUTER JOIN can be replaced by INNER JOIN
118.description: Using a `WHERE` condition on the right table of a `LEFT OUTER JOIN` effectively turns it into an `INNER JOIN`.
118.example:
SELECT s.cName, t.sName
FROM
    customer c
    LEFT OUTER JOIN store s ON c.cID = s.sID
WHERE s.city = 'Genoa';
118.explanation: The query uses a `LEFT OUTER JOIN` to join the `customer` and `store` tables, but the `WHERE` clause removes any `NULL` values from the right table, which means that only matching rows will be returned. This effectively turns the `LEFT OUTER JOIN` into an `INNER JOIN`, making the use of `LEFT OUTER JOIN` unnecessary in this context.
118.correction:
SELECT c.cName, s.sName
FROM
    customer c
    INNER JOIN store s ON c.cID = s.sID
WHERE s.city = 'Genoa';

119.name: Unused CTE
119.description: A Common Table Expression (CTE) is defined in the query but not referenced or used in the main query or any subqueries.
119.explanation: The CTE named `cte` is defined but never used in the main query. This adds unnecessary complexity to the query without providing any benefit.
